ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw minced pork as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for raw meat preparations is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw serving of minced pork is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a palm-sized portion used in stir-fries, dumplings, or meatball preparations.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database — Minced Pork, Raw (cr_id F06090012); supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Ground Pork, Raw (FDC ID 168352 / NDB 10219)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrient values (energy 203 kcal, protein 25.9 g, fat 11.0 g, carbohydrate 0.0 g, sugar 0.0 g, sodium 59 mg, dietary fibre 0.0 g, potassium 350 mg, calcium 12 mg, iron 1.22 mg, cholesterol 57 mg) are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for minced pork, raw (cr_id F06090012, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat (3.9 g), monounsaturated fat (4.71 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.05 g), trans fat (0.0 g), and all micronutrient values not reported by HPB (vitamins B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, C, A, D, E; folate; phosphorus; magnesium; zinc; selenium; water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Ground Pork, Raw (FDC ID 168352). Both HPB and USDA are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Minced pork, raw contains 0.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F06090012) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168352). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value is assigned per Veritas q4 rules for zero-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for pure protein/fat foods with zero carbohydrate content, consistent with this determination.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (59 mg/100 g) and high potassium (350 mg/100 g) yield a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Raw minced pork is low in sodium and rich in potassium, making it a suitable protein source for hypertensive individuals when prepared without high-sodium marinades, sauces, or preserved condiments; choose lean mince to limit saturated fat, which at higher intakes may contribute to arterial stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content; high protein (25.9 g/100 g) supports satiety and blunts postprandial glucose excursions
type2_diabetes.guidance: Minced pork contains no carbohydrate or sugar and therefore does not raise blood glucose; it is an appropriate protein source for people with Type 2 Diabetes, provided it is cooked without high-sugar sauces such as hoisin, sweet soy, or char siu marinade, and portion size is moderated to limit saturated fat intake that may worsen insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (3.9 g/100 g) and dietary cholesterol (57 mg/100 g) require portion moderation in the context of a heart-healthy diet
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Minced pork provides moderate saturated fat (3.9 g/100 g) and cholesterol (57 mg/100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit red and processed meat to no more than 70 g cooked per day per NHS/BHF guidance, choose lean mince, and balance intake with omega-3-rich foods to maintain a favourable polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid (omega-6) content in red meat may amplify pro-inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA disease activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat including pork to no more than 1–2 servings per week, and when consumed, pair with anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger, turmeric, or cruciferous vegetables to partially offset the pro-inflammatory omega-6 load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Minced pork, raw (also known as ground pork) is fresh pork that has been mechanically ground or finely chopped to produce a pale pink, soft, and malleable meat product. It is one of the most widely used raw meat ingredients across Asian and Western cuisines, appearing in Chinese dumplings (jiaozi, siu mai), Singaporean lion's head meatballs, Thai larb, Vietnamese bún bò, Italian ragù, and countless stir-fry and stuffed-vegetable preparations. Nutritionally, raw minced pork is a high-protein food providing 25.9 g protein per 100 g, with moderate fat (11.0 g) of which approximately 3.9 g is saturated fat. It contains zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, making it glycaemically inert and suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium content is low at 59 mg/100 g in the raw, unseasoned state, and potassium is relatively high at 350 mg/100 g, supporting a favourable DASH-diet sodium-to-potassium ratio. It is a meaningful source of B vitamins (thiamine 0.53 mg, niacin 5.37 mg, B12 0.69 mcg), zinc (2.39 mg), selenium (24 mcg), and iron (1.22 mg). Clinically, it is appropriate for people with Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension when prepared without added salt or sugar, but should be consumed in moderation by individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat and arachidonic acid content. It must be cooked thoroughly to an internal temperature of at least 71 °C (160 °F) to eliminate Salmonella, Listeria, and other pathogens associated with raw pork.
common_user_queries: How much protein is in minced pork?
Is ground pork high in fat?
Is minced pork suitable for hypertension?
Can I eat minced pork if I have high blood pressure?
Is raw minced pork low carb?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06090012, confidence 0.95) and supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168352) for comprehensive micronutrient and fatty acid profiles. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2. No estimation or analogy required for core macros.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06090012) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168352) for raw unseasoned pork mince; this is naturally zero as pork contains no plant-derived carbohydrate. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as pork is an animal product containing no dietary fibre. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw ground pork, which lists negligible trans fat in unprocessed pork. Monounsaturated_fat_g (4.71 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.05 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 168352 as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions; these are adapted values from an approved source. Saturated_fat_g (3.9 g) is an HPB-consistent estimate cross-validated against USDA (3.88 g). All remaining micronutrient values (vitamins, minerals) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 168352 as supplementary data where HPB does not report them. Halal status is marked false as pork is not halal by definition and no halal certification applies. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g per 100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null per Veritas q4 rules for low/zero-carbohydrate foods.
